interview_name: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins

main_summary.summary: Joint interview recorded May 28, 2011 in Bogalusa, Louisiana, with Geraldine Crawford Bennett, her niece Toni Breaux (Willie Exposé Breaux), and her nephew Willie Elliot Jenkins (known as Chuck). The three recall the Bogalusa civil rights movement through the work of Gayle Jenkins, Bennett's sister and the mother of Breaux and Jenkins. Gayle Jenkins served as secretary-treasurer of the Bogalusa Voters League alongside president A.Z. Young and vice-president Robert Hicks, and worked closely with CORE organizers and the Deacons for Defense during the 1965 campaign. The witnesses describe segregated life in Poplas Quarters, school integration through the Willie E. Jenkins v. Bogalusa School Board suit that placed Chuck in Bogalusa Junior High in fall 1967, the senior-class walkout at Central Memorial High in 1965, the 1967 march from Bogalusa to Baton Rouge with violence at Satsuma, Klan attacks on the Jenkins home, Gayle Jenkins's twenty-seven-year tenure on the Bogalusa City School Board, and a fifty-thousand-dollar buyout offer from Governor John McKeithen that Jenkins refused. Bennett practiced as a nurse in Covington for twenty-seven years after Bogalusa hospitals refused to hire her.
key_themes: women's organizing in the Black church tradition
main_summary.historical_significance: Direct family testimony about Gayle Jenkins, a central but under-documented organizer of the Bogalusa movement, provided by her sister, daughter, and son. The interview supplements the better-known accounts of A.Z. Young, Robert Hicks, and Charles Sims by foregrounding the women's organizing infrastructure behind the public male leadership and the household cost of sustained Klan threats. Specific named referents include the 1967 Bogalusa-to-Baton Rouge march at Satsuma, the school integration suit Willie E. Jenkins v. Bogalusa School Board, attorney Richard Sobol's legal work, the New Orleans CORE-affiliated law firm of Collins, Douglas, and Elie, and Governor John McKeithen's buyout offer.

chapters.title: Introductions and Pre-Movement Bogalusa
chapters.summary: Joe Mosnier introduces the recording for the Civil Rights History Project at the Jenkins family home in Bogalusa on May 28, 2011. Bennett describes pre-movement segregation in Bogalusa, work at the white-only Desport Clinic where she and her sister Gayle were maid and cook, and Gayle's determination to send her daughter to college.

chapters.title: Gayle Jenkins's Origins and Family
chapters.summary: Breaux recalls her mother Gayle Jenkins's biracial background, the absent white grandfather Mr. Smith in Poplas Quarters, and Gayle's refusal of political bribes to leave the movement. She describes being sent to live with her Aunt Sis during the most violent period because Klan threats made the Jenkins home unsafe for children.

chapters.title: Central Memorial Senior Walkout 1965
chapters.summary: Breaux describes the 1965 senior-class walkout at Central Memorial High School, with students marching to Freedom Hall on Sullivan Drive singing 'We Shall Overcome' over the objections of principal J.C. Crump. The walkout connected to her brother Willie E. Jenkins v. Bogalusa School Board, the suit that integrated the public schools.
related_events: Willie E. Jenkins v. Bogalusa School Board

chapters.title: Family Life and Gayle Jenkins's Businesses
chapters.summary: Breaux and Bennett describe Gayle Jenkins as domineering and a chance-taker who ran a hair salon, nursing practice, a store, a snowball stand, and rental houses. Bennett tells how Gayle was repeatedly turned down for nursing programs before completing training in New Orleans, then was barred from working in Bogalusa hospitals and commuted to Covington for her entire nursing career.

chapters.title: Monroe Jenkins and Entry into the Movement
chapters.summary: Bennett recounts that Monroe Jenkins, Gayle's husband, brought the family into the movement after a Friday-night confrontation in which he held a pocketknife to a Klansman's throat to prevent the killing of CORE workers on Fourth Street. The Deacons for Defense subsequently escorted Monroe to the paper mill because of threats on his life, and a white man attempting to kill him in the family store was driven off when Monroe drew a gun from beneath the counter.

chapters.title: Local Geography and the Voters League Transition
chapters.summary: The witnesses situate the Jenkins, Hicks, and Young homes in Bogalusa's Black neighborhoods (Poplas Quarters, Richardson Town, the Bogue Projects, River Terrace) relative to the Freedom House on Sullivan Drive. They describe the January 1965 transition in Voters League leadership when Andrew Moses of the NAACP resigned and Gayle Jenkins approached A.Z. Young to take over the presidency, with Robert Hicks as vice-president and Gayle herself as secretary-treasurer.

chapters.title: The Deacons for Defense in Bogalusa
chapters.summary: Jenkins describes the Deacons for Defense gathering at the family home, naming Charles and Roy Sims, Sam Barnes, Fletcher Anderson, and Bill Crawford (Bennett's father and the witnesses' grandfather). The Deacons positioned men at the front and sides of marches with women in the middle, returned gunfire on a car that fired on Monroe Jenkins driving his children home from a Freedom House meeting, and drove white vendors out of the Black quarters.

chapters.title: Gayle Jenkins on the School Board
chapters.summary: Breaux describes Gayle Jenkins's twenty-seven-year tenure on the Bogalusa City School Board, including an incident in which Jenkins drew a .38 from her purse and laid it on the table during a confrontation with the mother of a Genco family member running for superintendent. Breaux portrays her mother's profanity, her willingness to help white people who were being mistreated, and her decades-long withholding of the words 'I love you' from her own children.
related_events: Bogalusa school board confrontation with Genco family

chapters.title: 1965 Marches and Bogalusa-to-Baton Rouge
chapters.summary: Breaux remembers a Klansman pulling a gun on A.Z. Young near the old junior high, and Hattie Mae Hill being sprayed with a fire hose that lifted her dress during a Columbia Road march. Jenkins describes the march down Columbia Road where police ran over the white attacker Applewhite as he charged with a crowbar. Bennett recounts the 1967 march to Baton Rouge starting with twenty-five or thirty people and reaching five thousand strong, with violence at Satsuma where Don Duane Exposé and other marchers used straight razors stolen from Albany barbershops to cut Klansmen jumping over cars.

chapters.title: Don Duane Exposé and School Integration
chapters.summary: Breaux describes her late brother Don Duane Exposé, who led the first student walkout when she was a senior and Don was a junior, as more militant than their nonviolent mother. Jenkins recounts his integration of Bogalusa Junior High in fall 1967 under principal Mr. Flemings, who had announced on the radio that 'niggers' were invading the schools. Jenkins describes beating a white student who attacked him with a crutch and the principal's response that Jenkins should not let anyone know he had carried a pocketknife.
keywords: Willie E. Jenkins v. Bogalusa School Board

chapters.title: Post-Movement Employment Retaliation
chapters.summary: Breaux describes superintendent M.J. Israel's hostility when she applied for her first teaching job in Bogalusa, her decades of being passed over for principalships despite a master's-plus-thirty credential, and the role of board member Applewhite (whose family she names as Klan-affiliated) in blocking her rehiring after retirement. She recounts confronting Applewhite during a board meeting after he degraded the district's first Black superintendent.

chapters.title: Lawyers, Visitors, and Daily Marches
chapters.summary: The witnesses recall attorney Richard Sobol's frequent presence at the Jenkins home and the New Orleans CORE-affiliated law firm of Robert Collins, Nils Douglas, and Lolis Elie, who offered Breaux a Tulane scholarship she declined after three days. They describe James Farmer being smuggled out of Bogalusa in a casket from Richmond's funeral home, Dick Gregory addressing police at Sweetville Baptist Church when the lights were cut, and the twice-daily marches that proceeded with or without city permits.
related_events: Dick Gregory Sweetville Baptist Church speech

chapters.title: Final Reflections on Gayle Jenkins
chapters.summary: The witnesses describe Gayle Jenkins's refusal of Governor John McKeithen's fifty-thousand-dollar buyout offer and her counter-demand that every Black woman in Bogalusa receive a job. Breaux recounts state senator Ben Nevers seeking Gayle's support twice, Gayle's friendship with the openly racist board member Mr. Creel, and Gayle's decision shortly before her December 13 death to burn all her civil rights memorabilia in her backyard. The interview closes with reflections on attorney Richard Sobol's unfinished book project about Gayle and on her stated belief that education was the central goal of the movement.
related_events: Gayle Jenkins's burning of movement papers

loc_video.caption: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Bogalusa, Louisiana, 2011 May 28
